Sinners think living freely means being slaves to their desires, which is actually the worst kind of slavery. They turned away from God to worship false gods. 

When people get hot and thirsty, they drink cool, refreshing water. Yet people won't give up sure things for uncertain ones, yet Israel abandoned the safe path set by God’s laws. 

They didn’t stay on the safe road where they could travel without danger. Instead, they chose dangerous paths, just like those who follow false gods or sin. This made their land empty and them unhappy.

We can handle troubles if God helps us through them. But if He turns His back and doesn’t support us, we’re lost. Many people today forget about God and Jesus, leaving the old, good ways for new paths they create. What will happen to them on Judgment Day?

18.o: When the prophet called to repentance, instead of obeying the call, the people devised devices against him. Thus do sinners deal with the great Intercessor, crucifying him afresh, and speaking against him on earth, while his blood is speaking for them in heaven. But the prophet had done his duty to them; and the same will be our rejoicing in a day of evil.
18.m: When the prophet told people to turn away from their sins, they came up with ways to harm him instead. Sinners often treat the one who pleads for them badly, even though his blood speaks in their favor before God. The prophet did what he was supposed to do for them. And someday, that will bring us joy, even on a difficult day.

19.o: The prophet must give notice of ruin coming upon Judah and Jerusalem. Both rulers and ruled must attend to it. That place which holiness made the joy of the whole earth, sin made the reproach and shame of the whole earth. There is no fleeing from God's justice, but by fleeing to his mercy.
19.m: The prophet needs to warn about trouble coming to Judah and Jerusalem. Leaders and regular people should both listen carefully. A place that was once full of holiness and brought joy to everyone now brings shame because of sin. You cannot escape God’s judgment, but you can find safety by turning to his mercy.

19.o: The potter's vessel, after it is hardened, can never be pieced again when it is broken. And as the bottle was broken, so shall Judah and Jerusalem be broken by the Chaldeans. No human hand can repair it; but if they return to the Lord he will heal. As they filled Tophet with the slain sacrificed to their idols, so will God fill the whole city with the slain that shall fall as sacrifices to his justice. Whatever men may think, God will appear as terrible against sin and sinners as the Scriptures state; nor shall the unbelief of men make his promise or his threatenings of no effect. The obstinacy of sinners in sinful ways, is their own fault; if they are deaf to the word of God, it is because they have stopped their ears. We have need to pray that God, by his grace, would deliver us from hardness of heart, and contempt of his word and commandments.
19.m:
A potter’s clay, once baked hard, cannot be fixed if it breaks. Just as a bottle shatters, so too will Judah and Jerusalem break under attack by the Chaldeans. No one can mend them, but if they turn back to the Lord, He will heal them. 

They filled Tophet with dead bodies offered to false gods, and now God will fill the entire city with dead bodies as punishment for their sins. People might doubt, but God will show His power just as the scriptures describe. Neither people’s disbelief nor stubbornness can stop God’s promises or threats from coming true.

If people ignore God’s word, it’s because they’ve chosen to block out what they hear. We should ask God to soften our hearts through His grace, so we don’t despise His commands.

20.o: Pashur smote Jeremiah, and put him in the stocks. Jeremiah was silent till God put a word into his mouth. To confirm this, Pashur has a name given him, "Fear on every side." It speaks a man not only in distress, but in despair; not only in danger, but in fear on every side. The wicked are in great fear where no fear is, for God can make the most daring sinner a terror to himself. And those who will not hear of their faults from God's prophets, shall be made to hear them from their consciences. Miserable is the man thus made a terror to himself. His friends shall fail him. God lets him live miserably, that he may be a monument of Divine justice.
20.m:
Pashur hit Jeremiah and put him in the stocks. Jeremiah stayed quiet until God gave him words to speak. 

To show what would happen next, Pashur got a new name: "Terror All Around." This means he would be in trouble and afraid all the time, not just sometimes. Wicked people get scared even when there’s nothing to be afraid of because God can make anyone feel terrified. 

Those who won’t listen to God’s messengers will have to deal with feeling guilty inside instead. It’s terrible to be so scared that you’re a burden to yourself. Even his friends will abandon him. God allows him to suffer so everyone sees how He punishes wrongdoers.

20.o: The prophet complains of the insult and injury he experienced. But Jer. 20:7 may be read, Thou hast persuaded me, and I was persuaded. Thou wast stronger than I; and didst overpower me by the influence of thy Spirit upon me. So long as we see ourselves in the way of God, and of duty, it is weakness and folly, when we meet with difficulties and discouragements, to wish we had never set out in it. The prophet found the grace of God mighty in him to keep him to his business, notwithstanding the temptation he was in to throw it up. Whatever injuries are done to us, we must leave them to that God to whom vengeance belongs, and who has said, I will repay. So full was he of the comfort of God's presence, the Divine protection he was under, and the Divine promise he had to depend upon, that he stirred up himself and others to give God the glory. Let the people of God open their cause before Him, and he will enable them to see deliverance.
20.m:
The prophet talks about the trouble and harm he faced. In Jeremiah 20:7, it says, "Thou hast convinced me, and I was convinced." You were too strong for me, and you overpowered me through your Spirit. When we think we're doing what God wants and we face problems, it’s foolish to regret starting. 

Even though the prophet felt tempted to stop his work, he kept going because of God's help. We should trust God to handle any wrongs done to us, since He promises to take care of those things. The prophet felt so much joy from God’s presence, His protection, and the promises He made, that he encouraged both himself and others to praise God. People who follow God should tell their story to Him, and He’ll help them see hope and rescue.

20.o: When grace has the victory, it is good to be ashamed of our folly, to admire the goodness of God, and be warned to guard our spirits another time. See how strong the temptation was, over which the prophet got the victory by Divine assistance! He is angry that his first breath was not his last. While we remember that these wishes are not recorded for us to utter the like, we may learn good lessons from them. See how much those who think they stand, ought to take heed lest they fall, and to pray daily, Lead us not into temptation. How frail, changeable, and sinful is man! How foolish and unnatural are the thoughts and wishes of our hearts, when we yield to discontent! Let us consider Him who endured the contradiction of sinners against himself, lest we should be at any time weary and faint in our minds under our lesser trials.
20.m:
When we see God’s kindness win out, it’s good to feel embarrassed about our mistakes, to appreciate His goodness, and to be careful not to repeat the same errors.

Notice how strong the temptation was, yet the prophet won because God helped him. The prophet wished he had died instead of living through such trouble. We shouldn’t copy those kinds of wishes. Instead, we can learn important lessons from them.

This shows us how people who think they’re safe need to watch themselves closely so they don’t slip up. They should pray often, asking God not to let them face big tests.

How weak and changeable humans are! Our thoughts become silly and wrong when we get upset. Remember Jesus, who faced many hard things people said about him. This helps us stay strong even when we have small troubles.

21.o: When the siege had begun, Zedekiah sent to ask of Jeremiah respecting the event. In times of distress and danger, men often seek those to counsel and pray for them, whom, at other times, they despise and oppose; but they only seek deliverance from punishment. When professors continue in disobedience, presuming upon outward privileges, let them be told that the Lord will prosper his open enemies against them. As the king and his princes would not surrender, the people are exhorted to do so. No sinner on earth is left without a Refuge, who really desires one; but the way of life is humbling, it requires self-denial, and exposes to difficulties.
21.m:
When the city was under attack, King Zedekiah asked Jeremiah what would happen next. During hard times, people often turn to others for advice and prayers, even if they didn't respect them before. They just want help avoiding trouble.

Sometimes religious leaders ignore God’s commands. Then they wonder why their enemies seem to have an easier time. 

The king and his officers refused to give up. But the common people were encouraged to surrender. Anyone who truly wants to find safety can. It means being humble and willing to face challenges.

21.o: The wickedness of the king and his family was the worse because of their relation to David. They were urged to act with justice, at once, lest the Lord's anger should be unquenchable. If God be for us, who can be against us? But if he be against us, who can do any thing for us?
21.m: The evil actions of the king and his family were even worse because they were related to David. They needed to act justly right away, before God became really angry. If God is on our side, who can be against us? But if God is against us, who can help us?

22.o: The king of Judah is spoken to, as sitting upon the throne of David, the man after God's own heart. Let him follow his example, that he may have the benefit of the promises made to him. The way to preserve a government, is to do the duty of it. But sin will be the ruin of the houses of princes, as well as of meaner men. And who can contend with destroyers of God's preparing? God destroys neither persons, cities, nor nations, except for sin; even in this world he often makes it plain for what crimes he sends punishment; and it will be clear at the day of judgement.
22.m:
The king of Judah is addressed while sitting on David’s throne, which belonged to a man loved by God. He should follow David’s example to receive the blessings promised to him. Doing one’s duties helps keep a government strong. Sin harms royal families just as much as ordinary people. Who can fight against those chosen by God to punish? 

God doesn’t harm anyone unless they’ve sinned. In this life, he shows us why he sends punishments. On judgment day, everything will be clear.

22.o: Here is a sentence of death upon two kings, the wicked sons of a very pious father. Josiah was prevented from seeing the evil to come in this world, and removed to see the good to come in the other world; therefore, weep not for him, but for his son Shallum, who is likely to live and die a wretched captive. Dying saints may be justly envied, while living sinners are justly pitied. Here also is the doom of Jehoiakim. No doubt it is lawful for princes and great men to build, beautify, and furnish houses; but those who enlarge their houses, and make them sumptuous, need carefully to watch against the workings of vain-glory. He built his houses by unrighteousness, with money gotten unjustly. And he defrauded his workmen of their wages. God notices the wrong done by the greatest to poor servants and labourers, and will repay those in justice, who will not, in justice, pay those whom they employ. The greatest of men must look upon the meanest as their neighbours, and be just to them accordingly. Jehoiakim was unjust, and made no conscience of shedding innocent blood. Covetousness, which is the root of all evil, was at the bottom of all. The children who despise their parents' old fashions, commonly come short of their real excellences. Jehoiakim knew that his father found the way of duty to be the way of comfort, yet he would not tread in his steps. He shall die unlamented, hateful for oppression and cruelty.
22.m:
Here is a sentence of death on two kings, the bad sons of a very good father. Josiah was saved from seeing the troubles ahead in this life and taken to enjoy the goodness in the next life. So do not cry for him, but pity his son Shallum, who might live and die as an unhappy prisoner. A dying saint can be envied, while a living sinner should be pitied. 

Jehoiakim’s fate is also mentioned here. It is okay for leaders and important people to build and decorate houses. But those who make their homes fancy need to guard against pride. Jehoiakim used unfair means to get money and build his house. He cheated workers out of their pay too. God sees when rich people mistreat poor laborers and will punish those who don’t treat their employees fairly.

Even powerful people must treat humble workers kindly. Jehoiakim was dishonest and killed innocent people. His greed caused much harm. Children often reject their parents’ wise ways and end up worse off. Jehoiakim knew his dad found doing right brought true happiness, but he did not follow that path. Jehoiakim will die without anyone mourning him because he was hated for being cruel and oppressive.

22.o: The Jewish state is described under a threefold character. Very haughty in a day of peace and safety. Very fearful on alarm of trouble. Very much cast down under pressure of trouble. Many never are ashamed of their sins till brought by them to the last extremity. The king shall close his days in bondage. Those that think themselves as signets on God's right hand, must not be secure, but fear lest they should be plucked thence. The Jewish king and his family shall be carried to Babylon. We know where we were born, but where we shall die we know not; it is enough that our God knows. Let it be our care that we die in Christ, then it will be well with us wherever we die, thought it may be in a far country. The Jewish king shall be despised. Time was when he was delighted in; but all those in whom God has no pleasure, some time or other, will be so lowered, that men will have no pleasure in them. Whoever are childless, it is the Lord that writes them so; and those who take no care to do good in their days, cannot expect to prosper. How little is earthly grandeur to be depended upon, or flourishing families to be rejoiced in! But those who hear the voice of Christ, and follow him, have eternal life, and shall never perish, neither shall any enemy pluck them out of his almighty hands.
22.m:
The Jewish people had three main traits. They were very proud when things were going well. They got scared easily when there was trouble coming. And they felt really low when they faced real problems. 

Many people only feel bad about their wrongdoings once they face big consequences. The king would end up in slavery at the end of his life. People shouldn't get too comfortable thinking they're special to God. They need to stay humble and remember they could lose their favored position anytime.

The Jewish king and his family would be taken away to Babylon. We know where we were born, but we don’t always know where we’ll die. It’s enough to know that God knows. Our goal should be to die believing in Christ, because then everything will be okay no matter where we go, even if it’s somewhere far from home.

The Jewish king would be looked down upon. Once, people loved him, but now, since God isn’t pleased with him anymore, others won’t love him either. Anyone without children is written that way by God. People who don’t work to do good during their lives can’t expect to succeed.

How unreliable is power and wealth? Or having a successful family? But those who listen to Jesus and follow him have eternal life. They will never be lost, and no one can take them out of God’s strong hands.

23.o: Woe be to those who are set to feed God's people, but take no concern to do them good! Here is a word of comfort to the neglected sheep. Though only a remnant of God's flock is left, he will find them out, and they shall be brought to their former habitations. Christ is spoken of as a branch from David's family. He is righteous himself, and through him all his people are made righteous. Christ shall break the usurped power of Satan. All the spiritual seed of believing Abraham and praying Jacob shall be protected, and shall be saved from the guilt and dominion of sin. In the days of Christ's government in the soul, the soul dwells at ease. He is here spoken of as "the Lord our Righteousness." He is so our Righteousness as no creature could be. His obedience unto death is the justifying righteousness of believers, and their title to heavenly happiness. And their sanctification, as the source of all their personal obedience is the effect of their union with him, and of the supply of this Spirit. By this name every true believer shall call him, and call upon him. We have nothing to plead but this, Christ has died, yea, rather is risen again; and we have taken him for our Lord. This righteousness which he has wrought out to the satisfaction of law and justice, becomes ours; being a free gift given to us, through the Spirit of God, who puts it upon us, clothes us with it, enables us to lay hold upon it, and claim an interest in it. "The Lord our Righteousness" is a sweet name to a convinced sinner; to one that has felt the guilt of sin in his conscience; seen his need of that righteousness, and the worth of it. This great salvation is far more glorious than all former deliverances of his church. May our souls be gathered to Him, and be found in him.
23.m:
Trouble comes to those who are supposed to care for God’s people but don’t actually help them. There is hope for the ones who were forgotten. Even if only a small number of God’s people remain, he will still find them and bring them back to where they once lived.

Christ is part of David’s family line. He is righteous, and because of him, all his followers become righteous too. Christ will defeat Satan’s control over people. The children of faith from Abraham and prayer from Jacob will be kept safe and freed from the power of sin.

When Christ rules in someone’s heart, that person feels secure and at peace. He is called “the Lord our Righteousness.” Only Christ can give us such perfect righteousness. Through his obedience and sacrifice, he gives believers the right to heaven. And by connecting with him, believers grow holy and obey God better.

True followers call on him using this name, “the Lord our Righteousness,” and trust in him. Our only defense is that Christ died and rose again. We accept him as our Lord. The righteousness he earned satisfies God’s laws, and we receive it freely through the Holy Spirit. The Spirit helps us believe in it and use it.

This name means a lot to people who know they’ve done wrong. It shows how valuable Christ’s righteousness is. This salvation is greater than any other rescue God has ever provided. Let our hearts turn to Christ and stay close to him.

23.o: The false prophets of Samaria had deluded the Israelites into idolatries; yet the Lord considered the false prophets of Jerusalem as guilty of more horrible wickedness, by which the people were made bold in sin. These false teachers would be compelled to suffer the most bitter part of the Lord's indignation. They made themselves believe that there was no harm in sin, and practised accordingly; then they made others believe so. Those who are resolved to go on in evil ways, will justly be given up to believe strong delusions. But which of them had received any revelation of God, or understood any thing of his word? There was a time coming when they would reflect on their folly and unbelief with remorse. The teaching and example of the true prophets led men to repentance, faith, and righteousness. The false prophets led men to rest in forms and notions, and to be quiet in their sins. Let us take heed that we do not follow unrighteousness.
23.m:
The fake prophets in Samaria tricked the Israelites into worshipping idols. But the Lord saw the fake prophets in Jerusalem as even worse because they encouraged people to sin boldly. 

These false teachers would face the worst punishment from God. They convinced themselves that nothing bad would happen if they sinned, and acted that way. Then they told others the same lie. People who insist on doing wrong will rightly be left to believe big lies.

But did any of these false prophets get real messages from God or truly understand His word? One day, they would regret their foolish beliefs and lack of trust in God.

True prophets taught people to turn away from sin, have faith, and live righteously. False prophets led people to think that following certain rituals and ideas was enough, letting them stay comfortable in their sins.

We should be careful not to follow wrongdoing.

23.o: Men cannot be hidden from God's all-seeing eye. Will they never see what judgments they prepare for themselves? Let them consider what a vast difference there is between these prophecies and those delivered by the true prophets of the Lord. Let them not call their foolish dreams Divine oracles. The promises of peace these prophets make are no more to be compared to God's promises than chaff to wheat. The unhumbled heart of man is like a rock; if not melted by the word of God as a fire, it will be broken to pieces by it as a hammer. How can they be long safe, or at all easy, who have a God of almighty power against them? The word of God is no smooth, lulling, deceitful message. And by its faithfulness it may certainly be distinguished from false doctrines.
23.m:
People can't hide from God’s all-seeing eyes. Will they ever realize the trouble they’re bringing on themselves? Think about how different these fake prophecies are from the ones real prophets give. Don’t mistake your silly ideas for God’s messages. 

The peaceful promises made by these false prophets aren’t even close to God’s promises. It’s like comparing straw to wheat. A stubborn heart is like a hard rock. If God’s word doesn’t soften it like fire, it will break it apart like a hammer. 

How can anyone feel safe or comfortable when God, who has all power, is against them? God’s word isn’t soft and comforting. It tells the truth and sets itself apart from lies.

23.o: Those are miserable indeed who are forsaken and forgotten of God; and men's jesting at God's judgments will not baffle them. God had taken Israel to be a people near to him, but they shall now be cast out of his presence. It is a mark of great and daring impiety for men to jest with the words of God. Every idle and profane word will add to the sinner's burden in the day of judgment, when everlasting shame will be his portion.
23.m:
People are truly unhappy when God abandons and forgets them. Making fun of God’s punishments won’t help them. 

God once chose Israel to be close to him, but now they will be rejected by him. 

It shows great disrespect when people joke about what God says. 

Every careless and disrespectful word adds to a person’s guilt on Judgment Day, bringing eternal shame.

25.o: The call to turn from evil ways to the worship and service of God, and for sinners to trust in Christ, and partake of his salvation, concerns all men. God keeps an account how long we possess the means of grace; and the longer we have them, the heavier will our account be if we have not improved them. Rising early, points out the earnest desire that this people should turn and live. Personal and particular reformation must be insisted on as necessary to a national deliverance; and every one must turn from his own evil way. Yet all was to no purpose. They would not take the right and only method to turn away the wrath of God.
25.m:
The call to stop doing bad things and start worshipping and serving God applies to everyone. Sinners need to put their faith in Christ and receive his salvation. 

God notices how long we have had chances to grow closer to him. The more time we have had these chances, the bigger our responsibility becomes if we don't use them well.

Getting up early shows how much someone wants to change and start living better. Each person needs to fix their own mistakes first before there can be real improvement in the nation.

But none of this helped. People refused to do what was needed to ease God’s anger.

25.o: The fixing of the time during which the Jewish captivity should last, would not only confirm the prophecy, but also comfort the people of God, and encourage faith and prayer. The ruin of Babylon is foretold: the rod will be thrown into the fire when the correcting work is done. When the set time to favour Zion is come, Babylon shall be punished for their iniquity, as other nations have been punished for their sins. Every threatening of the Scripture will certainly be accomplished.
25.m:
Setting the time for when the Jewish people would return from captivity would not only prove the prophecy was true, but also give them hope and encourage them to keep praying. 

Babylon’s destruction is predicted. Once the punishment is over, the ruler’s power will end. When the right time comes to help Jerusalem, Babylon will face consequences for its wrongdoings, just as other countries faced consequences for theirs. 

Every warning in the Scriptures will definitely happen.

25.o: The evil and the good events of life are often represented in Scripture as cups. Under this figure is represented the desolation then coming upon that part of the world, of which Nebuchadnezzar, who had just began to reign and act, was to be the instrument; but this destroying sword would come from the hand of God. The desolations the sword should make in all these kingdoms, are represented by the consequences of excessive drinking. This may make us loathe the sin of drunkenness, that the consequences of it are used to set forth such a woful condition. Drunkenness deprives men of the use of their reason, makes men as mad. It takes from them the valuable blessing, health; and is a sin which is its own punishment. This may also make us dread the judgments of war. It soon fills a nation with confusion. They will refuse to take the cup at thy hand. They will not believe Jeremiah; but he must tell them it is the word of the Lord of hosts, and it is in vain for them to struggle against Almighty power. And if God's judgments begin with backsliding professors, let not the wicked expect to escape.
25.m:
In the Bible, good and bad things in life are often compared to cups. Here, the troubles coming to different countries because of King Nebuchadnezzar are described using this image. He was starting his rule and would cause destruction sent by God. 

The damage caused by the sword is shown through the effects of too much drinking. This might help us see how harmful drunkenness is since its results show a very sad state. Drinking too much takes away people’s ability to think clearly and makes them act crazy. It harms their health and punishes them right away.

This comparison could also make us fear the chaos of war. War quickly turns a country upside down. People won’t want to accept the message Jeremiah brings. But he has to tell them it comes directly from the Lord of Heaven’s armies. There’s no point fighting against God’s mighty power.

If God starts punishing those who have turned away from him, we shouldn’t think the wicked will get off easy.

25.o: The Lord has just ground of controversy with every nation and every person; and he will execute judgment on all the wicked. Who can avoid trembling when God speaks in displeasure? The days are fully come; the time fixed in the Divine counsels, which will make the nations wholly desolate. The tender and delicate shall share the common calamity. Even those who used to live in peace, and did nothing to provoke, shall not escape. Blessed be God, there is a peaceable habitation above, for all the sons of peace. The Lord will preserve his church and all believers in all changes; for nothing can separate them from his love.
25.m:
God has reasons to judge every nation and every person. He will punish all the wicked people. Who wouldn't feel scared when God shows anger?

The day has come that was planned by God. It will destroy all the nations completely. Even the weak and gentle people will suffer. Those who lived peacefully without causing trouble won't be spared either.

Thankfully, there is a safe place in heaven for all peaceful people. God protects his church and everyone who believes in him through all kinds of changes. Nothing can ever separate them from God’s love.

26.o: God's ambassadors must not seek to please men, or to save themselves from harm. See how God waits to be gracious. If they persisted in disobedience, it would ruin their city and temple. Can any thing else be expected? Those who will not be subject to the commands of God, make themselves subject to the curse of God.
26.m: God's messengers should not try to please people or protect themselves from trouble. Look at how God wants to show kindness. If they keep disobeying, it will destroy their city and temple. What else could happen? People who won't follow God's rules put themselves under God's punishment.

26.o: The priests and prophets charged Jeremiah as deserving death, and bore false witness against him. The elders of Israel came to inquire into this matter. Jeremiah declares that the Lord sent him to prophesy thus. As long as ministers keep close to the word they have from God, they need not fear. And those are very unjust who complain of ministers for preaching of hell and damnation; for it is from a desire to bring them to heaven and salvation. Jeremiah warns them of their danger if they go on against him. All men may know, that to hurt, or put to death, or to show hatred to their faithful reprovers, will hasten and increase their own punishment.
26.m: The priests and prophets said Jeremiah deserved death and told lies about him. The leaders of Israel looked into this case. Jeremiah said the Lord sent him to speak these words. As long as ministers stay true to what God tells them, they don’t need to be afraid. People are unfair if they get upset at ministers for talking about hell and punishment because the ministers want to help people reach heaven and safety. Jeremiah warned them that continuing to oppose him would bring them trouble. Everyone can see that hurting, killing, or hating those who tell the truth will only make their own punishments worse.

26.o: When secure sinners are threatened with taking away the Spirit of God, and the kingdom of God, it is what is warranted from the word of God. Hezekiah who protected Micah, prospered. Did Jehoiakim, who slew Urijah, prosper? The examples of bad men, and the bad consequences of their sins, should deter from what is evil. Urijah was faithful in delivering his message, but faulty in leaving his work. And the Lord was pleased to permit him to lose his life, while Jeremiah was protected in danger. Those are safest who most simply trust in the Lord, whatever their outward circumstances may be; and that He has all men's hearts in his hands, encourages us to trust him in the way of duty. He will honour and recompense those who show kindness to such as are persecuted for his sake.
26.m:
When people who think they're safe are warned about losing the Holy Spirit and the kingdom of heaven, it’s based on what the Bible says. Hezekiah, who helped Micah, did well. But did Jehoiakim, who killed Urijah, do well? Examples of bad people and the problems caused by their sins should stop others from doing wrong.

Urijah was honest in sharing his message but made a mistake by stopping his work. The Lord allowed Urijah to die, but he kept Jeremiah safe from harm. People are safest when they fully trust the Lord, even if things look tough around them. Since God controls everyone’s hearts, we can trust Him to help us do our duties. He rewards those who are kind to people who are treated badly because of their faith.

27.o: Jeremiah is to prepare a sign that all the neighbouring countries would be made subject to the king of Babylon. God asserts his right to dispose of kingdoms as he pleases. Whatever any have of the good things of this world, it is what God sees fit to give; we should therefore be content. The things of this world are not the best things, for the Lord often gives the largest share to bad men. Dominion is not founded in grace. Those who will not serve the God who made them, shall justly be made to serve their enemies that seek to ruin them. Jeremiah urges them to prevent their destruction, by submission. A meek spirit, by quiet submission to the hardest turns of providence, makes the best of what is bad. Many persons may escape destroying providences, by submitting to humbling providences. It is better to take up a light cross in our way, than to pull a heavier on our own heads. The poor in spirit, the meek and humble, enjoy comfort, and avoid many miseries to which the high-spirited are exposed. It must, in all cases, be our interest to obey God's will.
27.m:
Jeremiah needs to show a sign that nearby countries would become part of the kingdom of Babylon. 

God has the power to decide who rules over different lands. Anything people have in this life comes from God’s choice. We should accept whatever He gives us.

The best things aren’t always the material goods of this world. God often blesses wicked people with wealth and success.

Being powerful doesn't mean someone is good or faithful. People who won’t follow the God who created them might end up serving their enemies instead.

Jeremiah tells them to stop their destruction by submitting to Babylon. 

A gentle attitude helps even in tough situations. By calmly accepting hard times, you can find peace.

Sometimes, if you submit to small troubles, you can avoid bigger ones later. It’s smarter to deal with little problems now rather than face big disasters later.

Those who stay humble and peaceful usually have less trouble. They enjoy inner peace while others struggle.

In every situation, following God’s wishes is always best.

27.o: Jeremiah persuades the king of Judah to surrender to the king of Babylon. Is it their wisdom to submit to the heavy iron yoke of a cruel tyrant, that they may secure their lives; and is it not much more our wisdom to submit to the pleasant and easy yoke of our Lord and Master, Jesus Christ, that we may secure our souls? It were well if sinners would be afraid of the destruction threatened against all who will not have Christ to reign over them. Why should they die the second death, infinitely worse than that by sword and famine, when they may submit and live? And those who encourage sinners to go on in sinful ways, will perish with them.
27.m:
Jeremiah tells the king of Judah to give up to the king of Babylon. Is it smart for people to accept the harsh rule of a mean ruler just to save their lives? Isn't it even smarter to accept the gentle rule of Jesus Christ so we can save our souls?

It would be good if sinners were scared of the punishment promised to those who won’t let Christ rule over them. Why should they face the terrible second death, which is far worse than dying from war or hunger, when they could choose to follow Christ and live?

Those who tell others to keep sinning will suffer the same fate as the sinners.

27.o: Jeremiah assures them that the brazen vessels should go after the golden ones. All shall be carried to Babylon. But he concludes with a gracious promise, that the time would come when they should be brought back. Though the return of the prosperity of the church does not come in our time, we must not despair, for it will come in God's time.
27.m: Jeremiah tells them that the bronze items will follow the gold ones to Babylon. Everything will be taken there. But he ends on a hopeful note, saying that one day all these things will be returned. Even if the church’s good times don’t happen during our lives, we shouldn’t lose hope because it will happen eventually, in God’s timing.

28.o: Hananiah spoke a false prophecy. Here is not a word of good counsel urging the Jews to repent and return to God. He promises temporal mercies, in God's name, but makes no mention of the spiritual mercies which God always promised with earthly blessings. This was not the first time Jeremiah had prayed for the people, though he prophesied against them. He appeals to the event, to prove Hananiah's falsehood. The prophet who spake only of peace and prosperity, without adding that they must not by wilful sin stop God's favours, will be proved a false prophet. Those who do not declare the alarming as well as the encouraging parts of God's word, and call men to repentance, and faith, and holiness, tread in the steps of the false prophets. The gospel of Christ encourages men to do works meet for repentance, but gives no encouragement to continue in sin.
28.m:
Hananiah gave a false prophecy. He did not advise the Jews to turn back to God and ask forgiveness. Instead, he promised them good things would happen, but said nothing about the spiritual help that God usually offers along with material blessings.

This wasn’t the first time Jeremiah had asked God to bless the people, even though he often warned them too. 

Jeremiah pointed out what would happen to show that Hananiah’s prediction was wrong. A prophet who talks only about peace and success without warning that sins could stop God’s kindness is proven to be lying. 

Those who don’t tell both sides of God’s message—both the scary warnings and the hopeful promises—and don’t urge people to change their ways, follow the path of fake prophets. 

The good news about Jesus encourages us to live better lives, but it doesn’t let us keep doing wrong things.

28.o: Hananiah is sentenced to die, and Jeremiah, when he has received direction from God, boldly tells him so; but not before he received that commission. Those have much to answer for, who tell sinners that they shall have peace, though they harden their hearts in contempt of God's word. The servant of God must be gentle to all men. He must give up even his right, and leave the Lord to plead his cause. Every attempt of ungodly men to make vain the purposes of God, will add to their miseries.
28.m:
Hananiah is told he will die, and Jeremiah shares this message after receiving instructions from God. People who tell others they'll be fine when they ignore God’s warnings will face serious consequences. 

The person serving God should be kind to everyone. They might need to let go of their rights and trust God to handle things. When evil people try to stop God’s plans, it only makes their problems worse.

29.o: The written word of God is as truly given by inspiration of God as his spoken word. The zealous servant of the Lord will use every means to profit those who are far off, as well as those who are near him. The art of writing is very profitable for this end; and by the art of printing it is rendered most beneficial for circulating the knowledge of the word of God. God's sending to the captives by this letter would show that he had not forsaken them, though he was displeased, and corrected them. If they live in the fear of God, they may live comfortably in Babylon. In all conditions of life, it is our wisdom and duty not to throw away the comfort of what we may have, because we have not all we would have. They are directed to seek the good of the country where they were captives. While the king of Babylon protected them, they must live quiet and peaceable lives under him, in all godliness and honesty; patiently leaving it to God to work deliverance for them in due time.
29.m:
The written words from God are just as inspired as his spoken words. A dedicated follower of the Lord will do everything possible to help people learn, whether they are nearby or far away. Writing helps spread knowledge, and printing makes it even easier to share God’s message widely. By sending a letter to the captives, God showed that he still cared about them, even if he was punishing them. As long as they feared God, they could find comfort in Babylon. 

In any situation, we should make the best of what we have instead of focusing on what we lack. Those in captivity were told to look out for the welfare of their new land. When the king of Babylon kept them safe, they needed to live peacefully and honestly there, trusting God to free them at the right time.

29.o: Let men beware how they call those prophets whom they choose after their own fancies, and how they consider their fancies and dreams to be revelations from God. False prophets flatter people in their sins, because they love to be flattered; and they speak smoothly to their prophets, that their prophets may speak smoothly to them. God promises that they should return after seventy years were accomplished. By this it appears, that the seventy years of the captivity are not to be reckoned from the last captivity, but the first. It will be the bringing to pass of God's good word to them. This shall form God's purposes. We often do not know our own minds, but the Lord is never at an uncertainty. We are sometimes ready to fear that God's designs are all against us; but as to his own people, even that which seems evil, is for good. He will give them, not the expectations of their fears, or the expectations of their fancies, but the expectations of their faith; the end he has promised, which will be the best for them. When the Lord pours out an especial spirit of prayer, it is a good sign that he is coming toward us in mercy. Promises are given to quicken and encourage prayer. He never said, Seek ye me in vain. Those who remained at Jerusalem would be utterly destroyed, notwithstanding what the false prophets said to the contrary. The reason has often been given, and it justifies the eternal ruin of impenitent sinners; Because they have not hearkened to my words; I called, but they refused.
29.m:
People need to be careful about calling someone a prophet just because that person agrees with their ideas. False prophets tell people what they want to hear so they'll keep listening to them. 

God promised that the people would come back home after seventy years had passed. This means we count the time from when the first group was taken away, not the last one. 

This promise shows that God’s plans will happen. Sometimes we don’t know what we really want, but God always knows. People often think God wants bad things for them, but for his followers, even what seems bad turns out to be good.

The Lord will give them what they hope for through faith, not what they fear or imagine. When God sends a special spirit of praying, it means he is getting ready to show mercy. His promises help us pray more and feel encouraged. God never tells people to look for him without giving them hope.

Those who stayed in Jerusalem would be completely destroyed, even though the fake prophets said otherwise. This happens because they did not listen to God’s words. God spoke to them, but they ignored him.

29.o: Jeremiah foretells judgments upon the false prophets, who deceived the Jews in Babylon. Lying was bad; lying to the people of the Lord, to delude them into a false hope, was worse; but pretending to rest their own lies upon the God of truth, was worst of all. They flattered others in their sins, because they could not reprove them without condemning themselves. The most secret sins are known to God; and there is a day coming when he will bring to light all the hidden works of darkness. Shemaiah urges the priests to persecute Jeremiah. Their hearts are wretchedly hardened who justify doing mischief by having power to do it. They were in a miserable thraldom for mocking the messengers of the Lord, and misusing his prophets; yet in their distress they trespass still more against the Lord. Afflictions will not of themselves cure men of their sins, unless the grace of God works with them. Those who slight the blessings, deserve to lose the benefit of God's word, like Shemaiah. The accusations against many active Christians in all ages, amount to no more than this, that they earnestly counsel men to attend to their true interest and duties, and to wait for the performance of God's promises in his appointed way.
29.m:
Jeremiah warned about punishments for false prophets who tricked the Jews while in Babylon. Lying was wrong, but telling lies to deceive God’s people gave them false hope, which was even worse. Claiming those lies came from God made it the worst sin of all. These prophets told others what they wanted to hear so they wouldn’t have to admit their own sins. 

God knows all secrets. One day He will reveal everything hidden in darkness. Shemaiah urged the priests to hurt Jeremiah. People who think they can do evil just because they have power are very wicked. Mocking God’s messengers and mistreating His prophets brought great trouble. Even then, these people continued to disobey God.

Suffering alone won't fix someone’s sins unless God helps them. People who ignore God’s blessings deserve to miss out on His teachings, like Shemaiah did. Many good Christians through history faced unfair attacks. But the real charge was always that they tried to guide people toward right actions and waiting patiently for God’s promises.

30.o: Jeremiah is to write what God had spoken to him. The very words are such as the Holy Ghost teaches. These are the words God ordered to be written; and promises written by his order, are truly his word. He must write a description of the trouble the people were now in, and were likely to be in. A happy end should be put to these calamities. Though the afflictions of the church may last long, they shall not last always. The Jews shall be restored again. They shall obey, or hearken to the Messiah, the Christ, the Son of David, their King. The deliverance of the Jews from Babylon, is pointed out in the prophecy, but the restoration and happy state of Israel and Judah, when converted to Christ their King, are foretold; also the miseries of the nations before the coming of Christ. All men must honour the Son as they honour the Father, and come into the service and worship of God by him. Our gracious Lord pardons the sins of the believer, and breaks off the yoke of sin and Satan, that he may serve God without fear, in righteousness and true holiness before him all the remainder of his days, as the redeemed subject of Christ our King.
30.m:
Jeremiah was told to write down everything God spoke to him. The exact words came directly from the Holy Spirit. God commanded these specific messages to be written, so they are truly God’s words.

He needed to describe the troubles the people were facing at that time and would face in the future. But there would eventually be an end to those hardships. Even though the church might suffer for a long time, it won’t go on forever. 

The Jewish people will return home one day. They will listen to the Messiah, who is the Christ, the son of David and their king. This prophecy talks about the Jews being freed from Babylon, but it also predicts their return and happiness under Christ as their ruler. It also describes the suffering other nations will experience before the arrival of Christ.

Everyone must honor the Son just as they honor the Father. People need to follow God through Jesus. When someone becomes a believer, Jesus forgives their sins and frees them from the control of evil. Then they can serve God without fear, living righteously until the end of their life as subjects of Christ, our King.

30.o: When God is against a people, who will be for them? Who can be for them, so as to do them any kindness? Incurable griefs are owing to incurable lusts. Yet, though the captives suffered justly, and could not help themselves, the Lord intended to appear for them, and to punish their oppressors; and he will still do so. But every effort to heal ourselves must prove fruitless, so long as we neglect the heavenly Advocate and sanctifying Spirit. The dealings of His grace with every true convert, and every returning backslider, are the same in effect as his proceedings to the Jews.
30.m:
When God is against a people, who can be for them? Who can help them?

The sadness and troubles of the captives were because of their own desires. Even though they deserved what they got and couldn't help themselves, the Lord planned to help them and punish those who hurt them. He still does this today.

Trying to fix ourselves won’t work if we ignore the help from heaven. 

How God treats each person who turns to him—whether new believers or those coming back to faith—is the same as how he treated the Jewish people.

30.o: We have here further intimations of the favour of God for them after the days of their calamity have expired. The proper work and office of Christ, as Mediator, is to draw near unto God, for us, as the High Priest of our profession. His own undertaking, in compliance with his Father's will, and in compassion to fallen man, engaged him. Jesus Christ was, in all this, truly wonderful. They shall be taken again into covenant with the Lord, according to the covenant made with their fathers. "I will be your God:" it is his good-will to us, which is the summary of that part of the covenant. The wrath of God against the wicked is very terrible, like a whirlwind. The purposes of his wrath, as well as the purposes of his love, will all be fulfilled. God will comfort all that turn to him; but those who approach him must have their hearts engaged to do it with reverence, devotion, and faith. How will they escape who neglect so great salvation?
30.m:
Here we see more signs that God will show favor to them after their difficult times end. 

Christ’s role as our Mediator is to come close to God on our behalf, just like a High Priest would. He did this because he wanted to follow his Father’s wishes and out of compassion for people who have sinned. 

Jesus Christ acted wonderfully in all of this. 

They will once again enter into an agreement with the Lord, just like the one made with their ancestors. “I will be your God” summarizes the loving part of that agreement. 

God’s anger towards sinners is very scary, like a violent storm. Both his plans for punishment and his plans for mercy will happen. 

God will give comfort to everyone who turns to him. But those who come to him must do so with respect, devotion, and faith. Those who ignore such a great offer of salvation cannot avoid its consequences.

31.o: God assures his people that he will again take them into covenant relation to himself. When brought very low, and difficulties appear, it is good to remember that it has been so with the church formerly. But it is hard under present frowns to take comfort from former smiles; yet it is the happiness of those who, through grace, are interested in the love of God, that it is an everlasting love, from everlasting in the counsels, to everlasting in the continuance. Those whom God loves with this love, he will draw to himself, by the influences of his Spirit upon their souls. When praising God for what he has done, we must call upon him for the favours his church needs and expects. When the Lord calls, we must not plead that we cannot come; for he that calls us, will help us, will strengthen us. The goodness of God shall lead them to repentance. And they shall weep for sin with more bitterness, and more tenderness, when delivered out of their captivity, than when groaning under it. If we take God for our Father, and join the church of the first-born, we shall want nothing that is good for us. These predictions doubtless refer also to a future gathering of the Israelites from all quarters of the globe. And they figuratively describe the conversion of sinners to Christ, and the plain and safe way in which they are led.
31.m:
God promises to bring his people back into a close relationship with him. Even when things seem really tough, it helps to remember that the church has faced similar struggles before. It’s hard to find hope now when times are bad, but those who know God’s love can be happy because His love lasts forever—always there from beginning to end. 

Those God truly loves, He draws closer to Himself through the work of His Spirit. While thanking God for past blessings, we should ask Him for new favors the church needs. We shouldn’t say we can’t answer when God calls us; He’ll give us strength to respond. God’s kindness leads people to feel sorry for their sins. After being freed from trouble, they often cry harder over their mistakes than when they were suffering.

If we see God as our Father and belong to His family, we won’t lack anything good. This promise likely includes bringing Jewish people together from around the world someday. It also describes how sinners turn to Christ and follow a clear path to salvation.

31.o: He that scattered Israel, knows where to find them. It is comfortable to observe the goodness of the Lord in the gifts of providence. But our souls are never valuable as gardens, unless watered with the dews of God's Spirit and grace. A precious promise follows, which will not have full accomplishment except in the heavenly Zion. Let them be satisfied of God's loving-kindness, and they will be satisfied with it, and desire no more to make them happy. Rachel is represented as rising from her grave, and refusing to be comforted, supposing her offspring rooted out. The murder of the children at Bethlehem, by Herod, Matt. 2:16-18, in some degree fulfilled this prediction, but could not be its full meaning. If we have hope in the end, concerning an eternal inheritance, for ourselves and those belonging to us, all temporal afflictions may be borne, and will be for our good.
31.m:
He who scattered Israel knows where to find them. It’s comforting to see how God provides for his people. But our souls aren’t valuable unless they’re watered with God’s Spirit and grace. 

A wonderful promise comes next, which won't fully come true until the time of heaven. When people know God loves them, they’ll be content and want nothing else to make them happy.

Rachel is shown coming back from her grave, unwilling to be consoled because she thinks her children are gone forever. The killing of babies in Bethlehem by King Herod partly fulfilled this prophecy, but wasn’t what it meant completely. 

If we believe there’s a good ending waiting for us and those we love, then even hard times here on earth can be endured and will turn out well for us in the end.

31.o: Ephraim (the ten tribes) is weeping for sin. He is angry at himself for his sin, and folly, and frowardness. He finds he cannot, by his own power, keep himself close with God, much less bring himself back when he is revolted. Therefore he prays, Turn thou me, and I shall be turned. His will was bowed to the will of God. When the teaching of God's Spirit went with the corrections of his providence, then the work was done. This is our comfort in affliction, that the Lord thinks upon us. God has mercy in store, rich mercy, sure mercy, suitable mercy, for all who seek him in sincerity.
31.m:
Ephraim, which represents the ten tribes, is crying because of his sins. He is upset with himself for being sinful, foolish, and stubborn. He realizes he cannot stay close to God on his own, let alone come back after straying away. So he asks God to turn him around, and if God does, Ephraim will follow. 

His desires were aligned with what God wanted. When God’s Spirit taught him along with the lessons from life’s difficulties, the work was completed. This gives us hope during hard times because God cares about us. For those who sincerely look for Him, God has mercy ready—abundant, dependable, and fitting mercy.

31.o: The way from the bondage of sin to the liberty of God's children, is a high-way. It is plain, it is safe; yet none are likely to walk in it, unless they set their hearts towards it. They are encouraged by the promise of a new, unheard-of, extraordinary thing; a creation, a work of Almighty power; the human nature of Christ, formed and prepared by the power of the Holy Ghost: and this is here mentioned as an encouragement to the Jews to return to their own land. And a comfortable prospect is given them of a happy settlement there. Godliness and honesty God has joined: let no man think to put them asunder, or to make the one atone for the want of the other. In the love and favour of God the weary soul shall find rest, and the sorrowful shall find joy. And what can we see with more satisfaction than the good of Jerusalem, and peace upon Israel?
31.m:
The path from being trapped by sin to enjoying freedom as God’s children is straightforward and safe. But people won’t follow it unless they really want to. 

They’re told about a wonderful, amazing promise—a powerful act of creation. The Spirit made Jesus’ human body ready. This promise encourages Jewish people to go back to their homeland.

A bright future awaits them there. Godliness and honesty go together—don’t try to separate them. A tired soul will find rest in God’s love, and those who feel sad will be joyful.

What could bring us more happiness than seeing Jerusalem thrive and peace come to Israel?

31.o: The people of God shall become numerous and prosperous. In Heb. 8:8,9, this place is quoted as the sum of the covenant of grace made with believers in Jesus Christ. Not, I will give them a new law; for Christ came not to destroy the law, but to fulfil it; but the law shall be written in their hearts by the finger of the Spirit, as formerly written in the tables of stone. The Lord will, by his grace, make his people willing people in the day of his power. All shall know the Lord; all shall be welcome to the knowledge of God, and shall have the means of that knowledge. There shall be an outpouring of the Holy Spirit, at the time the gospel is published. No man shall finally perish, but for his own sins; none, who is willing to accept of Christ's salvation.
31.m:
The people of God will grow in number and do well. Hebrews 8:8,9 mentions this as the main part of the agreement of grace with those who believe in Jesus Christ. It’s not about giving them a new law because Christ didn’t come to get rid of the law but to fulfill it. Instead, the law will be written on their hearts by the Spirit, just as it was once written on stone tablets. 

God will use his grace to make his people willing followers on the day he acts. Everyone will know God; everyone will have access to knowing God and ways to learn about him. When the gospel is shared, there will be a pouring out of the Holy Spirit. No one will end up lost forever except because of their own sins. Anyone who wants to receive Christ’s salvation won’t be left behind.

31.o: As surely as the heavenly bodies will continue their settled course, according to the will of their Creator, to the end of time, and as the raging sea obeys him, so surely will the Jews be continued a separate people. Words can scarcely set forth more strongly the restoration of Israel. The rebuilding of Jerusalem, and its enlargement and establishment, shall be an earnest of the great things God will do for the gospel church. The personal happiness of every true believer, as well as the future restoration of Israel, is secured by promise, covenant, and oath. This Divine love passes knowledge; and to those who take hold upon it, every present mercy is an earnest of salvation.
31.m:
Just as the stars will keep moving across the sky forever, just as the ocean follows God’s command, so too will the Jewish people remain distinct. These words show clearly the return of the Israelites. Rebuilding Jerusalem, making it bigger and stronger, shows what God plans to do for his church. 

The promises, covenants, and oaths guarantee both the future restoration of Israel and the personal joy of each true believer. God’s love goes beyond understanding, and all the good things we receive now are signs of our future salvation.

32.o: Jeremiah, being in prison for his prophecy, purchased a piece of ground. This was to signify, that though Jerusalem was besieged, and the whole country likely to be laid waste, yet the time would come, when houses, and fields, and vineyards, should be again possessed. It concerns ministers to make it appear that they believe what they preach to others. And it is good to manage even our worldly affairs in faith; to do common business with reference to the providence and promise of God.
32.m:
Jeremiah bought some land while he was in jail because of his prophecies. This showed that even though Jerusalem was under attack and the area might get destroyed, there would be a time when people could live in houses, work their fields, and tend their vineyards again.

It’s important for teachers to show that they truly believe what they teach others. We should also handle our regular jobs with faith, doing daily tasks while thinking about God’s care and promises.

32.o: Jeremiah adores the Lord and his infinite perfections. When at any time we are perplexed about the methods of Providence, it is good for us to look to first principles. Let us consider that God is the fountain of all being, power, and life; that with him no difficulty is such as cannot be overcome; that he is a God of boundless mercy; that he is a God of strict justice; and that he directs every thing for the best. Jeremiah owns that God was righteous in causing evil to come upon them. Whatever trouble we are in, personal or public, we may comfort ourselves that the Lord sees it, and knows how to remedy it. We must not dispute God's will, but we may seek to know what it means.
32.m:
Jeremiah praises the Lord and his perfect qualities. Whenever we're confused about how things happen in the world, it helps to think about basic truths. Remember that God is the source of everything—life, power, and existence. He can overcome any challenge because there’s nothing too hard for him. He is full of mercy and always fair. 

Jeremiah admits that God was right to let bad things happen to people. Whether our problems are personal or affect everyone, we can find comfort knowing that the Lord sees everything and knows how to fix it. We shouldn’t question God’s plans, but we should try to understand what he wants us to learn from them.

32.o: God's answer discovers the purposes of his wrath against that generation of the Jews, and the purposes of his grace concerning future generations. It is sin, and nothing else, that ruins them. The restoration of Judah and Jerusalem is promised. This people were now at length brought to despair. But God gives hope of mercy which he had in store for them hereafter. Doubtless the promises are sure to all believers. God will own them for his, and he will prove himself theirs. He will give them a heart to fear him. All true Christians shall have a disposition to mutual love. Though they may have different views about lesser things, they shall all be one in the great things of God; in their views of the evil of sin, and the low estate of fallen man, the way of salvation through the Saviour, the nature of true holiness, the vanity of the world, and the importance of eternal things. Whom God loves, he loves to the end. We have no reason to distrust God's faithfulness and constancy, but only our own hearts. He will settle them again in Canaan. These promises shall surely be performed. Jeremiah's purchase was the pledge of many a purchase that should be made after the captivity; and those inheritances are but faint resemblances of the possessions in the heavenly Canaan, which are kept for all who have God's fear in their hearts, and do not depart from him. Let us then bear up under our trials, assured we shall obtain all the good he has promised us.
32.m:
God’s response shows why he was angry with that group of Jewish people and what good things he planned for future groups. Their problems came only because of their sins. God promised to bring back Judah and Jerusalem. Right now, these people felt hopeless. But God gave them hope by promising to help them later on. 

The promises God makes always come true for those who believe. God will show that he belongs to them and they belong to him. He will give them a heart to respect him. True followers of Christ will love each other. Even if they disagree on some small points, they will agree on big ideas like seeing sin as bad, knowing humans need saving, understanding real goodness, realizing the world isn’t perfect, and seeing eternal matters as important.

Those whom God loves, he keeps loving forever. We shouldn't doubt God’s trustworthiness, just our own hearts. God will let them live in Canaan again. These promises will definitely happen. When Jeremiah bought land, it showed that more people would buy land after they returned from being captives. Those earthly possessions are just shadows compared to the heavenly home waiting for everyone who fears God and stays faithful.

So, even during hard times, we can stay strong knowing God will keep his promises to us.

33.o: Those who expect to receive comforts from God, must call upon him. Promises are given, not to do away, but to quicken and encourage prayer. These promises lead us to the gospel of Christ; and in that God has revealed truth to direct us, and peace to make us easy. All who by sanctifying grace are cleansed from the filth of sin, by pardoning mercy are freed from the guilt. When sinners are thus justified, washed, and sanctified in the name of the Lord Jesus, and by the Holy Spirit, they are enabled to walk before God in peace and purity. Many are led to perceive the real difference between the people of God and the world around them, and to fear the Divine wrath. It is promised that the people who were long in sorrow, shall again be filled with joy. Where the Lord gives righteousness and peace, he will give all needful supplies for temporal wants; and all we have will be comforts, as sanctified by the word and by prayer.
33.m:
People who want to get comfort from God should ask him for it. God’s promises aren’t meant to stop us from praying, but to help us pray more. 

These promises guide us to the teachings of Jesus. In those teachings, God shows us what is true and helps us feel at ease. 

When God forgives our sins and makes us clean, we can live peaceful and pure lives. Those who are forgiven, cleaned, and made holy in the name of Jesus and by the Holy Spirit can walk with God in peace and holiness.

Many see the difference between God’s people and others. They learn to fear God’s anger.

God promises that people who suffered for a long time will now find joy. 

Where God gives us righteousness and peace, he will provide everything else we need. What we have becomes a comfort because it’s used in ways that honor God through his Word and prayer.

33.o: To crown the blessings God has in store, here is a promise of the Messiah. He imparts righteousness to his church, for he is made of God to us righteousness; and believers are made the righteousness of God in him. Christ is our Lord God, our righteousness, our sanctification, and our redemption. His kingdom is an everlasting kingdom. But in this world prosperity and adversity succeed each other, as light and darkness, day and night. The covenant of priesthood shall be secured. And all true believers are a holy priesthood, a royal priesthood, they offer up spiritual sacrifices, acceptable to God; themselves, in the first place, as living sacrifices. The promises of that covenant shall have full accomplishment in the gospel Israel. In Gal. 6:16, all that walk according to the gospel rule, are made to be the Israel of God, on whom shall be peace and mercy. Let us not despise the families which were of old the chosen people of God, though for a time they seem to be cast off.
33.m:
Here is a promise of the Messiah to bring even more blessings. He gives righteousness to his church because he makes us righteous before God. Believers become the righteousness of God through him. 

Christ is our Lord, our righteousness, our holiness, and our salvation. His kingdom will last forever. 

In this world, good times and hard times come one after another, just like day and night. The agreement about being priests will stay secure. True believers are a special group of priests who offer spiritual gifts that please God. They give their lives as offerings.

The promises from that agreement will fully come true for those who follow the rules of the gospel. As Paul writes in Galatians 6:16, anyone who follows the teachings of the gospel becomes part of God’s true Israel, where there is peace and mercy.

We should not look down on the families that were once God’s chosen people, even if they seem left out for a while.

34.o: Zedekiah is told that the city shall be taken, and that he shall die a captive, but he shall die a natural death. It is better to live and die penitent in a prison, than to live and die impenitent in a palace.
34.m:
Zedekiah is told that the city will be captured, and he will die while being held prisoner. But he will die of natural causes. 

It’s better to live and die feeling sorry for your sins in a prison than to live and die without regretting your sins in a palace.

34.o: A Jew should not be held in servitude above seven years. This law they and their fathers had broken. And when there was some hope that the siege was raised, they forced the servants they had released into their services again. Those who think to cheat God by dissembled repentance and partial reformation, put the greatest cheat upon their own souls. This shows that liberty to sin, is really only liberty to have the sorest judgments. It is just with God to disappoint expectations of mercy, when we disappoint the expectations of duty. And when reformation springs only from terror, it is seldom lasting. Solemn vows thus entered into, profane the ordinances of God; and the most forward to bind themselves by appeals to God, are commonly most ready to break them. Let us look to our hearts, that our repentance may be real, and take care that the law of God regulates our conduct.
34.m:
A Jewish owner could not keep someone in slavery for more than seven years. They broke this law, which both they and their ancestors knew about. When they thought the enemy might stop attacking, they made freed slaves work for them again.

People who try to trick God with fake remorse or half-hearted changes will fool themselves the most. Giving people freedom to sin actually means giving them the worst punishments. So if we don’t do what we’re supposed to, God won’t show us mercy either. 

When people only reform out of fear, their changes usually don’t last long. Making solemn promises while not truly changing harms God’s rules. The ones quickest to promise God they’ll follow the rules often end up breaking those promises too.

We need to check our hearts to ensure our regret is genuine. We must let God’s laws guide how we live.

35.o: Jonadab was famous for wisdom and piety. He lived nearly 300 years before, II Kin. 10:15. Jonadab charged his posterity not to drink wine. He also appointed them to dwell in tents, or movable dwelling: this would teach them not to think of settling any where in this world. To keep low, would be the way to continue long in the land where they were strangers. Humility and contentment are always the best policy, and men's surest protection. Also, that they might not run into unlawful pleasures, they were to deny themselves even lawful delights. The consideration that we are strangers and pilgrims should oblige us to abstain from all fleshly lusts. Let them have little to lose, and then losing times would be the less dreadful: let them sit loose to what they had, and then they might with less pain be stript of it. Those are in the best frame to meet sufferings who live a life of self-denial, and who despise the vanities of the world. Jonadab's posterity observed these rules strictly, only using proper means for their safety in a time of general suffering.
35.m:
Jonadab was known for being wise and religious. He lived almost 300 years earlier, according to II Kings 10:15. 

Jonadab told his family not to drink wine. He also made them live in tents instead of permanent homes. This taught them not to settle down anywhere in this world. Staying humble helped them stay safe in a place where they were foreigners. Being modest and happy with little was always smart and kept them protected.

To stop them from getting into trouble, he asked them to give up even some good things. Knowing they were just passing through should make people avoid worldly desires. Having few possessions meant they wouldn’t lose much during hard times. People who don’t hold onto many things can handle losses better. Self-control helps prepare someone to face difficulties.

Jonadab’s family followed these rules carefully. They used safe methods to protect themselves during tough times.

35.o: The trial of the Rechabites' constancy was for a sign; it made the disobedience of the Jews to God the more marked. The Rechabites were obedient to one who was but a man like themselves, and Jonadab never did for his seed what God has done for his people. Mercy is promised to the Rechabites. We are not told respecting the performance of this promise; but doubtless it was performed, and travellers say the Rechabites may be found a separate people to this day. Let us follow the counsels of our pious forefathers, and we shall find good in so doing.
35.m: The test of the Rechabites' loyalty was meant as a sign. It showed how much the Jews disobeyed God. The Rechabites followed someone who was just like them, while Jonadab never did for his family what God did for his people. A promise of mercy was given to the Rechabites. We don't know if the promise came true, but surely it did, and travelers still say you can find the Rechabites today as a separate group. Let’s listen to the advice of our godly ancestors, and we will see that it leads to good things.

36.o: The writing of the Scriptures was by Divine appointment. The Divine wisdom directed to this as a proper means; if it failed, the house of Judah would be the more without excuse. The Lord declares to sinners the evil he purposes to do against them, that they may hear, and fear, and return from their evil ways; and whenever any one makes this use of God's warnings, in dependence on his promised mercy, he will find the Lord ready to forgive his sins. All others will be left without excuse; and the consideration that great is the anger God has pronounced against us for sin, should quicken both our prayers and our endeavours.
36.m:
God chose who would write the Scriptures. He picked these people because He knew it would help others learn right from wrong. If it didn't work, those in Judah would have no excuse for their bad behavior.

When God tells sinners about the punishment coming their way, He wants them to listen, feel afraid, and stop doing wrong things. When someone uses God’s warnings to turn back to Him, asking for forgiveness, God is ready to forgive their sins.

Anyone else who ignores these warnings will have no excuse. Knowing that God is very angry at sin should make us pray more and try harder to avoid wrongdoing.

36.o: Shows of piety and devotion may be found even among those, who, though they keep up forms of godliness, are strangers and enemies to the power of it. The princes patiently attended the reading of the whole book. They were in great fear. But even those who are convinced to the truth and importance of what they hear, and are disposed to favour those who preach it, often have difficulties and reserves about their safety, interest, or preferment, so that they do not act according to their convictions, and try to get rid of what they find troublesome.
36.m:
People might show they are religious, even if they don’t really follow God’s teachings. The princes listened quietly while the whole book was read. They were very afraid. 

But sometimes people agree with what they hear and want to support those who teach it. Still, they worry about their safety, interests, or jobs. So they don’t always do what they know is right and try to avoid things that bother them.

36.o: Those who despise the word of God, will soon show, as this king did, that they hate it; and, like him, they would wish it destroyed. See what enmity there is against God in the carnal mind, and wonder at his patience. The princes showed some concern, till they saw how light the king made of it. Beware of making light of God's word!
36.m:
People who don't respect God’s word often end up showing that they really hate it, just like this king did. They might even want to get rid of it. It’s amazing how much people who aren’t spiritual dislike God. But notice how patient he remains.

The princes were worried at first, but then they saw how little the king cared about God’s word. Don’t treat God’s word lightly!

37.o: Numbers witness the fatal effects of other men's sins, yet heedlessly step into their places, and follow the same destructive course. When in distress, we ought to desire the prayers of ministers and Christian friends. And it is common for those to desire to be prayed for, who will not be advised; yet sinners are often hardened by a pause in judgments. But if God help us not, no creature can. Whatever instruments God has determined to use, they shall do the work, though they seem unlikely.
37.m:
Numbers show the bad results of other people’s mistakes, yet they carelessly follow the same path. 

When we’re in trouble, we should ask ministers and Christian friends to pray for us. 

It’s common for people to want others to pray for them even if they won’t listen to advice. Yet, sinners often become more stubborn when God delays judgment on them.

But without God’s help, nothing else can save us. 

Whatever tools God decides to use will get the job done, even if they seem unlikely at first.

37.o: There are times when it is the wisdom of good men to retire, to enter into their chambers, and to shut the doors, Is. 26:20. Jeremiah was seized as a deserter, and committed to prison. But it is no new thing for the best friends of the church to be belied, as in the interests of her worst enemies. When thus falsely accused, we may deny the charge, and commit our cause to Him who judges righteously. Jeremiah obtained mercy of the Lord to be faithful, and would not, to obtain mercy of man, be unfaithful to God or to his prince; he tells the king the whole truth. When Jeremiah delivered God's message, he spake with boldness; but when he made his own request, he spake submissively. A lion in God's cause must be a lamb in his own. And God gave Jeremiah favour in the eyes of the king. The Lord God can make even the cells of a prison become pastures to his people, and will raise up friends to provide for them, so that in the days of famine they shall be satisfied.
37.m:
Sometimes good people need to go inside and close themselves off from others, just like Isaiah said to do. Jeremiah got caught and put in jail because some people thought he was a traitor.
