But it’s not unusual for the church’s truest supporters to get wrongly blamed as if they were its biggest enemies. When you’re falsely accused, you can defend yourself and trust God to judge fairly.

Jeremiah stayed loyal to God and his king, even though he could have lied to save himself. He told the king everything honestly. 

When Jeremiah spoke God’s words, he did it boldly. But when he asked for personal favors, he was humble. In God’s work, we should be brave lions, but gentle lambs in other matters.

God helped Jeremiah win the king’s favor. Even in prison, God turned those cells into safe places for him. God finds ways to care for His people, providing for them during hard times.

38.o: Jeremiah went on in his plain preaching. The princes went on in their malice. It is common for wicked people to look upon God's faithful ministers as enemies, because they show what enemies the wicked are to themselves while impenitent. Jeremiah was put into a dungeon. Many of God's faithful witnesses have been privately made away in prisons. Ebed-melech was an Ethiopian; yet he spoke to the king faithfully, These men have done ill in all they have done to Jeremiah. See how God can raise up friends for his people in distress. Orders were given for the prophet's release, and Ebed-melech saw him drawn up. Let this encourage us to appear boldly for God. Special notice is taken of his tenderness for Jeremiah. What do we behold in the different characters then, but the same we behold in the different characters now, that the Lord's children are conformed to his example, and the children of Satan to their master?
38.m:
Jeremiah continued his straightforward preaching. The princes kept being mean. Wicked people often see God’s faithful servants as enemies because those servants tell them they’re hurting themselves by not changing their ways. 

Jeremiah was put in a pit. Many of God’s faithful messengers have been quietly gotten rid of in prison. Ebed-melech was from Ethiopia, but he told the king honestly, “These men have treated Jeremiah badly.” This shows how God can find helpers for His people who are in trouble. 

Orders were given to free the prophet, and Ebed-melech helped pull him out. This should give us courage to speak up for God. We see special kindness in how Ebed-melech cared about Jeremiah. Just like back then, today we see God’s children following His example and Satan’s children acting like their master.

38.o: Jeremiah was not forward to repeat the warnings, which seemed only to endanger his own life, and to add to the king's guilt, but asked whether he feared to do the will of God. The less men fear God, the more they fear men; often they dare not act according to their own judgments and consciences.
38.m:
Jeremiah did not want to keep warning people because it might put his life in danger and make King Josiah even more guilty. He wondered if the king was afraid to follow what God wanted him to do.

The less people fear God, the more they fear other people. Often, they don’t dare to do what they think is right because they are scared of what others might think or do to them.

39.o: Jerusalem was so strong, that the inhabitants believed the enemy could never enter it. But sin provoked God to withdraw his protection, and then it was as weak as other cities. Zedekiah had his eyes put out; so he was condemned to darkness who had shut his eyes against the clear light of God's word. Those who will not believe God's words, will be convinced by the event. Observe the wonderful changes of Providence, how uncertain are earthly possessions; and see the just dealings of Providence: but whether the Lord makes men poor or rich, nothing will profit them while they cleave to their sins.
39.m:
Jerusalem was very strong, and its people thought enemies could never get inside. But because of their sins, God stopped protecting them. Then Jerusalem became as weak as any other city.

Zedekiah had his eyes put out. This meant that someone who refused to see the truth in God’s word was now forced into darkness. People who won’t listen to what God says will find out the hard way through events that happen.

Notice how things can suddenly change due to God’s guidance. Wealth and power on earth aren’t reliable. Whether God makes someone poor or rich, their situation won't improve if they keep sinning.

39.o: The servants of God alone are prepared for all events; and they are delivered and comforted, while the wicked suffer. They often meet with more kindness from the profane, than from hypocritical professors of godliness. The Lord will raise them up friends, do them good, and perform all his promises.
39.m: God’s servants are ready for anything that happens. They find comfort and safety, while the wicked face trouble. Often, people who don’t follow religion treat God’s followers better than those who pretend to be religious. The Lord gives God’s people helpers and keeps all his promises to them.

39.o: Here is a message to assure Ebed-melech of a recompence for his great kindness to Jeremiah. Because thou hast put thy trust in me, saith the Lord. God recompenses men's services according to their principles. Those who trust God in the way of duty, as this good man did, will find that their hope shall not fail in times of the greatest danger.
39.m:
Here is a message to reassure Ebed-melech about getting rewarded for being kind to Jeremiah.

Because you trusted in me, says the Lord. 

God rewards people based on their beliefs. When someone trusts God while doing what’s right, like this good man did, they will discover that their hope won’t let them down even during the biggest dangers.

40.o: The captain of the guard seems to glory that he had been God's instrument to fulfil, what Jeremiah had been God's messenger to foretell. Many can see God's justice and truth with regard to others, who are heedless and blind as to themselves and their own sins. But, sooner or later, all men shall be made sensible that their sin is the cause of all their miseries. Jeremiah has leave to dispose of himself; but is advised to go to Gedaliah, governor of the land under the king of Babylon. It is doubtful whether Jeremiah acted right in this decision. But those who desire the salvation of sinners, and the good of the church, are apt to expect better times from slight appearances, and they will prefer the hope of being useful, to the most secure situations without it.
40.m:
The captain of the guard seems proud that he carried out what God told Jeremiah to predict. Many people notice God’s fairness and truth in other people’s lives, but ignore their own sins. Eventually, everyone will realize that their troubles come from their wrongdoings. 

Jeremiah gets to choose his own fate, but is advised to join Gedaliah, who was the leader of the land under the king of Babylon. We don’t know if Jeremiah made the right choice here. People who care about saving others and helping the church often think things might get better based on small signs. They would rather have the chance to help, even if it means less safety for them.

40.o: Jeremiah had never in his prophecies spoken of any good days for the Jews, to come immediately after the captivity; yet Providence seemed to encourage such an expectation. But how soon is this hopeful prospect blighted! When God begins a judgment, he will complete it. While pride, ambition, or revenge, bears rule in the heart, men will form new projects, and be restless in mischief, which commonly ends in their own ruin. Who would have thought, that after the destruction of Jerusalem, rebellion would so soon have sprung up? There can be no thorough change but what grace makes. And if the miserable, who are kept in everlasting chains for the judgment of the great day, were again permitted to come on earth, the sin and evil of their nature would be unchanged. Lord, give us new hearts, and that new mind in which the new birth consists, since thou hast said we cannot without it see thy heavenly kingdom.
40.m:
Jeremiah never talked about good times coming right after the Jews returned from being captives. Yet, things looked promising at first. But quickly, those hopes faded away. 

When God starts punishing people, He finishes what He started. As long as people are proud, ambitious, or full of anger, they keep making trouble. This often leads them to ruin themselves.

Who would have guessed that after Jerusalem was destroyed, there would still be rebellions so soon?

There can't be real change unless God changes our hearts. Even if someone very bad got another chance, they'd probably do the same wrong things again.

Lord, please give us new hearts and minds that match the new life you offer. Without that, we can’t enter your heavenly kingdom.

41.o: Those who hate the worshippers of God, often put on the appearance of piety, that they may the easier hurt them. As death often meets men where they least expect it, we should continually search whether we are in such a state and frame of mind, as we would wish to be found in when called to appear before our Judge. Sometimes the ransom of a man's life is his riches. But those who think to bribe death, saying, Slay us not, for we have treasures in the field, will find themselves wretchedly deceived. This melancholy history warns us, never to be secure in this world. We never can be sure of peace on this side heaven.
41.m:
People who dislike those who worship God sometimes pretend to be religious so they can more easily harm them. Death often comes at unexpected times, so we should always check if we are ready to meet our judge. 

Sometimes money can save someone’s life. But people who try to pay off death by saying, “Don’t kill us because we have treasure hidden in the fields,” will be sadly mistaken. This sad story reminds us never to feel safe in this world. We can never be certain of peace until we reach heaven.

41.o: The success of villany must be short, and none can prosper who harden their hearts against God. And those justly lose comfort in real fears, who excuse themselves in sin by pretended fears. The removal of a prudent and peaceable ruler, and the succession of another who is rash and ambitious, affects the welfare of many. Only those are happy and steady who fear the Lord and walk in his ways.
41.m: Bad people don't succeed for long. Those who refuse to listen to God will fail. People who hide behind fake worries to cover up their sins lose true peace. When a wise and peaceful leader steps down and someone reckless takes over, many suffer. True happiness comes only from those who respect God and follow his teachings.

42.o: To serve a turn, Jeremiah is sought out, and the captains ask for his assistance. In every difficult, doubtful case, we must look to God for direction; and we may still, in faith, pray to be guided by a spirit of wisdom in our hearts, and the leadings of Providence. We do not truly desire to know the mind of God, if we do not fully resolve to comply with it when we know it. Many promise to do what the Lord requires, while they hope to have their pride flattered, and their favourite lusts spared. Yet something betrays the state of their hearts.
42.m:
The leaders asked Jeremiah for help because they needed guidance. When faced with tough choices, we should seek God’s direction. We can pray for wisdom from Him and trust His leading.

We aren’t really seeking God’s will unless we’re ready to follow it once we learn what He wants us to do. Some people say they’ll do what God asks, hoping He won’t tell them to give up things they love. But their true feelings show through eventually.

42.o: If we would know the mind of the Lord in doubtful cases, we must wait as well as pray. God is ever ready to return in mercy to those he has afflicted; and he never rejects any who rely on his promises. He has declared enough to silence even the causeless fears of his people, which discourge them in the way of duty. Whatever loss or suffering we may fear from obedience, is provided against in God's word; and he will protect and deliver all who trust in him and serve him. It is folly to quit our place, especially to quit a holy land, because we meet with trouble in it. And the evils we think to escape by sin, we certainly bring upon ourselves. We may apply this to the common troubles of life; and those who think to avoid them by changing their place, will find that the grievances common to men will meet them wherever they go. Sinners who dissemble with God in solemn professions especially should be rebuked with sharpness; for their actions speak more plainly than words. We know not what is good for ourselves; and what we are most fond of, and have our hearts most set upon, often proves hurtful, and sometimes fatal.
42.m:
If we want to know God’s will in uncertain situations, we need to both pray and wait patiently. God is always willing to show mercy to those he has punished before. He never turns away anyone who trusts his promises. 

God has given us enough assurance to quiet the worries of his followers, which can hold them back from doing what is right. Any losses or hardships we might fear from obeying God are covered in his Word. He protects and saves everyone who puts their faith in him and serves him.

It’s foolish to leave our post just because we face problems, especially leaving a sacred place out of fear. The bad things we try to avoid through sinning actually come to us instead. 

We can relate this to the usual difficulties in life. People who think they can escape these troubles by moving around end up facing similar issues wherever they go. Those who pretend with God during serious commitments should be strongly corrected, since their actions reveal the truth better than words do. 

We don’t always know what’s best for us. Often, the things we desire most turn out to be harmful or even deadly.

43.o: Only by pride comes contention, both with God and man. They preferred their own wisdom to the revealed will of God. Men deny the Scriptures to be the word of God, because they are resolved not to conform themselves to Scripture rules. When men will persist in sin, they charge the best actions to bad motives. These Jews deserted their own land, and threw themselves out of God's protection. It is the folly of men, that they often ruin themselves by wrong endeavours to mend their situation.
43.m: Only pride leads to arguments, both with God and other people. They thought their own ideas were better than what God said. People reject the Bible as God’s word because they don’t want to follow its rules. When people keep doing wrong things, they say good actions have bad reasons behind them. The Jews left their own land and put themselves outside God’s protection. Often, people try to fix their problems in ways that end up hurting them instead.

43.o: God can find his people wherever they are. The Spirit of prophecy was not confined to the land of Israel. It is foretold that Nebuchadnezzar should destroy and carry into captivity many of the Egyptians. Thus God makes one wicked man, or wicked nation, a scourge and plague to another. He will punish those who deceive his professing people, or tempt them to rebellion.
43.m: God can find his people no matter where they are. The gift of prophecy wasn't limited to just the land of Israel. It was predicted that Nebuchadnezzar would destroy and take many Egyptians away as captives. So God uses one evil person or country to punish another. He punishes those who trick his followers or try to get them to rebel.

44.o: God reminds the Jews of the sins that brought desolations upon Judah. It becomes us to warn men of the danger of sin with all seriousness: Oh, do not do it! If you love God, do not, for it is provoking to him; if you love your own souls, do not, for it is destructive to them. Let conscience do this for us in the hour of temptation. The Jews whom God sent into the land of the Chaldeans, were there, by the power of God's grace, weaned from idolatry; but those who went by their own perverse will into the land of the Egyptians, were there more attached than ever to their idolatries. When we thrust ourselves without cause or call into places of temptation, it is just with God to leave us to ourselves. If we walk contrary to God, he will walk contrary to us. The most awful miseries to which men are exposed, are occasioned by the neglect of offered salvation.
44.m:
God reminded the Jews about the sins that caused trouble in Judah. We should warn people seriously about the dangers of sin: Don't do it! If you love God, don’t do it because it makes him angry. If you care about yourself, don’t do it because it’s harmful. Conscience should tell us this during times of temptation.

The Jews God sent to Babylon learned to stop worshipping idols. But those who went to Egypt on their own choice became even more devoted to false gods. When we put ourselves in tempting situations without reason, God might let us face our mistakes alone. 

If we go against God, he may go against us too. People suffer terrible things when they ignore God’s help.

44.o: These daring sinners do not attempt excuses, but declare they will do that which is forbidden. Those who disobey God, commonly grow worse and worse, and the heart is more hardened by the deceitfulness of sin. Here is the real language of the rebellious heart. Even the afflictions which should have parted them from their sins, were taken so as to confirm them in their sins. It is sad when those who should quicken each other to what is good, and so help one another to heaven, harden each other in sin, and so ripen one another for hell. To mingle idolatry with Divine worship, and to reject the mediation of Christ, are provoking to God, and ruinous to men. All who worship images, or honour saints, and angels, and the queen of heaven, should recollect what came from the idolatrous practices of the Jews.
44.m:
These bold sinners don't try to make excuses. They admit they will do what is forbidden. People who disobey God often get worse over time. Their hearts become harder because of sin’s trickery.

This shows the true words of someone who rebels against God. Even the troubles that should have made them stop sinning only made them cling to their sins even tighter. 

It's sad when people who could encourage each other to be good instead push each other deeper into sin. This makes them ready for hell together.

Mixing idols with true worship and rejecting Jesus’ role as our mediator really angers God and leads to destruction. Anyone who worships statues or honors saints, angels, or the Virgin Mary should remember what happened when the Jewish people practiced idolatry.

44.o: Whatever evil comes upon us, it is because we have sinned against the Lord; we should therefore stand in awe, and sin not. Since they were determined to persist in their idolatry, God would go on to punish them. What little remains of religion were among them, would be lost. The creature-comforts and confidences from which we promise ourselves most, may fail as soon as those from which we promise ourselves least; and all are what God makes them, not what we fancy them to be. Well-grounded hopes of our having a part in the Divine mercy, are always united with repentance and obedience.
44.m:
Whatever bad things happen to us come because we have sinned against the Lord. So we should be afraid and not sin anymore.

Since they kept doing their idol worship, God continued punishing them. 

The small amount of true religion left among them would disappear.

The comforts and support we think will always be there might suddenly vanish. They are only what God makes them, not what we imagine them to be.

Having real hope in God’s mercy goes hand in hand with being sorry for our sins and obeying him.

46.o: The whole word of God is against those who obey not the gospel of Christ; but it is for those, even of the Gentiles, who turn to Him. The prophecy begins with Egypt. Let them strengthen themselves with all the art and interest they have, yet it shall be all in vain. The wounds God inflicts on his enemies, cannot be healed by medicines. Power and prosperity soon pass from one to another in this changing world.
46.m:
The entire Bible is against people who do not follow the gospel of Christ. But it supports those who turn to him, even if they are Gentiles. 

The prophecy starts with Egypt. They can try to help themselves using all their skills and connections, but it will not work. Medicine cannot heal the injuries God gives to his enemies. In this ever-changing world, power and success quickly move from one person to another.

46.o: Those who encroached on others, shall now be themselves encroached on. Egypt is now like a very fair heifer, not accustomed to the yoke of subjection; but destruction comes out of the north: the Chaldeans shall come. Comfort and peace are spoken to the Israel of God, designed to encourage them when the judgments of God were abroad among the nations. He will be with them, and only correct them in measure; and will not punish them with everlasting destruction from his presence.
46.m:
Those who took what wasn’t theirs will now have others take from them. Egypt is like a beautiful cow that has never worn a yoke before. But now, destruction is coming from the north. The Chaldeans will attack.

Comfort and peace are promised to God’s people, the Israelites. This is meant to encourage them during times when God’s punishments are happening among other nations. God will stay with them and only discipline them in moderation. He won’t punish them with eternal destruction.

49.o: The Edomites were old enemies to the Israel of God. But their day is now at hand; it is foretold, not only to warn them, but for the sake of the Israel of God, whose afflictions were aggravated by them. Thus Divine judgments go round from nation to nation; the earth is full of commotion, and nothing can escape the ministers of Divine vengeance. The righteousness of God is to be observed amidst the violence of men.
49.m:
The Edomites had always been enemies of God’s people, the Israelites. Now their time has come. This warning is given not just for them, but also to remind God’s people that their troubles were made worse because of the Edomites. 

Divine punishments move from one nation to another. The whole world is in turmoil, and nothing escapes the agents of God’s justice. We should notice God’s fairness even when people act violently.

49.o: How easily God can dispirit those nations that have been most celebrated for valour! Damascus waxes feeble. It was a city of joy, having all the delights of the sons of men. But those deceive themselves who place their happiness in carnal joys.
49.m: God can quickly weaken even the strongest nations. Damascus has become weak. It used to be a city full of joy and pleasure. But people who think happiness comes from worldly pleasures are mistaken.

49.o: Nebuchadnezzar would make desolation among the people of Kedar, who dwelt in the deserts of Arabia. He who conquered many strong cities, will not leave those unconquered that dwell in tents. He will do this to gratify his own covetousness and ambition; but God orders it for correcting an unthankful people, and for warning a careless world to expect trouble when they seem most safe. They shall flee, get far off, and dwell deep in the deserts; they shall be dispersed. But privacy and obscurity are not always protection and security.
49.m:
Nebuchadnezzar would destroy the people of Kedar, who lived in the desert areas of Arabia. Even though he had already taken over many strong cities, he wouldn't ignore those living in tents. 

He did this out of greed and pride, but God allowed it to happen to teach a lesson to ungrateful people and warn others about unexpected troubles even when things seem safe.

The people of Kedar would run away, hide far from their homes, and live deep in the wilderness. They would scatter all around. However, hiding alone won’t always keep them safe.

49.o: The Elamites were the Persians; they acted against God's Israel, and must be reckoned with. Evil pursues sinners. God will make them know that he reigns. Yet the destruction of Elam shall not be for ever. But this promise was to have its full accomplishment in the days of the Messiah. In reading the Divine assurance of the destruction of all the enemies of the church, the believer sees that the issue of the holy war is not doubtful. It is blessed to recollect, that He who is for us, is more than all against us. And he will subdue the enemies of our souls.
49.m:
The Elamites were the Persians. They acted against God’s people Israel, and they would face consequences. Bad things happen to those who do wrong. God will show everyone that he is in charge. 

However, the punishment on Elam won’t last forever. This promise about their future came true during the time of the Messiah. When we read about God destroying all the enemies of his church, we see that the outcome is certain. 

It’s good to remember that whoever is on God’s side has more power than all the enemies combined. God will defeat the forces that try to harm us.

50.o: The king of Babylon was kind to Jeremiah, yet the prophet must foretell the ruin of that kingdom. If our friends are God's enemies, we dare not speak peace to them. The destruction of Babylon is spoken of as done thoroughly. Here is a word for the comfort of the Jews. They shall return to their God first, then to their own land; the promise of their conversion and reformation makes way for the other promises. Their tears flow not from the sorrow of the world, as when they went into captivity, but from godly sorrow. They shall seek after the Lord as their God, and have no more to do with idols. They shall think of returning to their own country. This represents the return of poor souls to God. In true converts there are sincere desires to attain the end, and constant cares to keep in the way. Their present case is lamented as very sad. The sins of professing Christians never will excuse those who rejoice in destroying them.
50.m:
The king of Babylon treated Jeremiah kindly, but the prophet had to warn about the downfall of that kingdom. Even if our friends are against God, we cannot tell them everything will be fine.

Babylon’s destruction would be complete. There is hope here for the Jewish people. First, they will turn back to God, and then they will go home. The promise of their turning to God comes before all the other good things promised to them.

Their tears won’t come from sadness over worldly troubles, like when they were taken away. Instead, these tears will show they feel sorry for sinning against God. They will look to God as their only true God and stop worshiping false gods. 

They will want to go back to their homeland. This shows how people return to God. True believers really want to reach their goal and stay on the right path. Right now, their situation seems very bad. But the sins of people who claim to follow Christ don't give others an excuse to celebrate when those followers suffer.

50.o: The desolation that shall be brought upon Babylon is set forth in a variety of expressions. The cause of this destruction is the wrath of the Lord. Babylon shall be wholly desolated; for she hath sinned against the Lord. Sin makes men a mark for the arrows of God's judgments. The mercy promised to the Israel of God, shall not only accompany, but arise from the destruction of Babylon. These sheep shall be gathered from the deserts, and put again into good pasture. All who return to God and their duty, shall find satisfaction of soul in so doing. Deliverances out of trouble are comforts indeed, when fruits of the forgiveness of sin.
50.m:
The destruction coming to Babylon will be described in many ways. This ruin happens because of God’s anger. Babylon will be completely destroyed since she has sinned against the Lord. Sin makes people targets for God’s punishments. 

Mercy will come to God’s people after Babylon is destroyed. The sheep will be brought back from the wilderness and placed in good pastures once more. Anyone who returns to God and their duties will find peace in their souls. Being freed from troubles feels wonderful when it comes as a result of God forgiving sins.

50.o: The forces are mustered and empowered to destroy Babylon. Let them do what God demands, and they shall bring to pass what he threatens. The pride of men's hearts sets God against them, and ripens them apace for ruin. Babylon's pride must be her ruin; she has been proud against the Holy One of Israel; who can keep those up whom God will throw down?
50.m: The forces are ready to destroy Babylon. They should do what God tells them to do and carry out his threats. People’s pride makes God their enemy and speeds up their destruction. Babylon’s pride will lead to its downfall. She was proud against the Holy One of Israel. Who can stop someone God wants to bring down?

50.o: It is Israel's comfort in distress, that, though they are weak, their Redeemer is strong. This may be applied to believers, who complain of the dominion of sin and corruption, and of their own weakness and manifold infirmities. Their Redeemer is able to keep what they commit to him; and sin shall not have dominion over them. He will give them that rest which remains for the people of God. Also here is Babylon's sin, and their punishment. The sins are, idolatry and persecution. He that will not save his people in their sins, never will countenance the wickedness of his open enemies. The judgments of God for these sins will lay them waste. In the judgments denounced against prosperous Babylon, and the mercies promised to afflicted Israel, we learn to choose to suffer affliction with the people of God, rather than to enjoy the pleasures of sin for a season.
50.m:
Israel has comfort even in times of trouble because their Redeemer is strong, even if they themselves are weak. 

This applies to believers who feel overwhelmed by sin and their own weaknesses. Their Redeemer is powerful enough to protect them and help them overcome sin. He gives them peace and rest.

Babylon’s sins include worshiping idols and persecuting others. God won’t forgive those who don’t turn from their sins, nor will he support those openly opposing him. 

God punishes Babylon for its sins by destroying it. We see this in the warnings about Babylon’s downfall and the promises of mercy for troubled Israel. From this, we learn to prefer suffering with God’s people instead of enjoying temporary pleasure through sin.

51.o: The particulars of this prophecy are dispersed and interwoven, and the same things left and returned to again. Babylon is abundant in treasures, yet neither her waters nor her wealth shall secure her. Destruction comes when they did not think of it. Wherever we are, in the greatest depths, at the greatest distances, we are to remember the Lord our God; and in the times of the greatest fears and hopes, it is most needful to remember the Lord. The feeling excited by Babylon's fall is the same with the New Testament Babylon, Rev. 18:9,19. The ruin of all who support idolatry, infidelity, and superstition, is needful for the revival of true godliness; and the threatening prophecies of Scripture yield comfort in this view. The great seat of antichristian tyranny, idolatry, and superstition, the persecutor of true Christians, is as certainly doomed to destruction as ancient Babylon. Then will vast multitudes mourn for sin, and seek the Lord. Then will the lost sheep of the house of Israel be brought back to the fold of the good Shepherd, and stray no more. And the exact fulfilment of these ancient prophecies encourages us to faith in all the promises and prophecies of the sacred Scriptures.
51.m:
This prophecy has details spread out and repeated. Babylon has lots of treasure, but neither her water supply nor her wealth will save her. Destruction will come when she least expects it. No matter where we are—no matter how deep in trouble or far from safety—we should always remember the Lord our God. In times of fear and hope, remembering the Lord is especially important.

People felt the same way about Babylon’s fall as they do about the New Testament’s “Babylon.” Destroying those who support false religions helps bring true godliness back. The threats in the Bible give us comfort because they show that places that hurt believers will one day be destroyed, just like old Babylon was.

When the big centers of wrong beliefs and persecution are destroyed, many people will feel sorry for their sins and look to the Lord. Lost people from Israel will return to the Good Shepherd and won’t wander away anymore. These old prophecies coming true gives us confidence in all the promises and prophecies in the Bible.

51.o: This prophecy is sent to Babylon, to the captives there, by Seraiah, who is to read it to his countrymen in captivity. Let them with faith see the end of these threatening powers, and comfort themselves herewith. When we see what this world is, how glittering its shows, and how flattering its proposals, let us read in the book of the Lord that it shall shortly be desolate. The book must be thrown into the river Euphrates. The fall of the New Testament Babylon is thus represented, Rev. 18:21. Those that sink under the weight of God's wrath and curse, sink for ever. Babylon, and every antichrist, will soon sink and rise no more for ever. Let us hope in God's word, and quietly wait for his salvation; then we shall see, but shall not share, the destruction of the wicked.
51.m:
This message was sent to Babylon to the people held captive there. Seraiah was supposed to read it to his fellow countrymen who were in captivity. They should have faith and see that the threats against their enemies would come true, giving them comfort.

When we look at the world today, seeing all its shiny promises and attractive offers, we should remember what the Lord’s book tells us—that it will soon become empty and deserted. 

Seraiah was told to throw the book into the River Euphrates. This act represents how the New Testament Babylon fell, as described in Revelation 18:21. People who are weighed down by God’s anger and punishment will sink forever. Babylon and everything like it will soon disappear and never rise again.

We should trust in God’s word and patiently wait for His salvation. Then we will see the destruction of evil without being part of it ourselves.

52.o: This fruit of sin we should pray against above any thing; Cast me not away from thy presence, Ps. 51:11. None are cast out of God's presence but those who by sin have first thrown themselves out. Zedekiah's flight was in vain, for there is no escaping the judgments of God; they come upon the sinner, and overtake him, let him flee where he will.
52.m:
We should pray most of all to avoid the results of sin; Don't throw me out of your presence, Psalm 51:11 says. People are only pushed away by God if they first push themselves away through their sins. 

Zedekiah tried to run away, but it didn't help. You cannot escape God's judgment. It finds you no matter where you go.

52.o: The Chaldean army made woful havoc. But nothing is so particularly related here, as the carrying away of the articles in the temple. The remembrance of their beauty and value shows us the more the evil of sin.
52.m: The Chaldean army caused great destruction. But what is described most specifically here is the taking of items from the temple. Remembering how beautiful and valuable these things were helps us see just how bad sin is.

52.o: The leaders of the Jews caused them to err; but now they are, in particular, made monuments of Divine justice. Here is an account of two earlier captivities. This people often were wonders both of judgment and mercy.
52.m: The Jewish leaders led people astray. Now they have become examples of God’s justice. There is a record of two earlier times when people were captured. This group was often seen as a wonder of both punishment and mercy.

52.o: See this history of king Jehoiachin in II Kin. 25:27-30. Those under oppression will find it is not in vain for them to hope and quietly to wait for the salvation of the Lord. Our times are in God's hand, for the hearts of all we have to deal with are so. May we be enabled, more and more, to rest on the Rock of Ages, and to look forward with holy faith to that hour, when the Lord will bring again Zion, and overthrow all the enemies of the church.
52.m:
Look at the story about King Jehoiachin in II Kings 25:27-30. People who are suffering should keep hoping and waiting quietly for God’s help. 

Our time is in God’s hands because He controls everyone else’s hearts too. Let’s learn to trust God more and more. We need to believe that one day, God will restore His people and defeat all their enemies.
